name: Mount Washington
dataSource: USGS, AMC, White Mountain National Forest, Mount Washington Avalanche Center; PeakBagger/SummitPost (route, class, hazards)
safetyNote: Tuckerman Ravine and nearby terrain are subject to avalanche forecasts, General Bulletins, and seasonal closures or route restrictions when snow, ice, undermined snow, waterfall holes, or avalanche conditions create unacceptable hazard. In spring, specific sections such as the Lip area of the Tuckerman Ravine Trail may be closed until snow has melted out. Check Mount Washington Avalanche Center and Pinkham Notch guidance before entering the ravine.

overview: Mount Washington stands 6,288 feet in the Presidential Range of northern New Hampshire, the highest point in the Northeast and one of the most famously exposed summits in the eastern United States. With roughly 6,148 feet of prominence, Mount Washington is one of the most topographically dominant peaks in the region. The peak is legendary for weather extremes: the summit recorded a 231 mph gust on April 12, 1934, recognized for decades as the world record for surface wind speed before being surpassed by a tropical-cyclone gust measured in Australia in 1996. Conditions can shift from benign to dangerous in minutes.

Geologically, Mount Washington consists of quartzite and mica schist bedrock exposed above treeline, creating a barren, windswept summit plateau. The Appalachian Trail crosses the summit, making Mount Washington one of the most prominent and weather-exposed summits on the AT corridor. Despite its modest absolute elevation, Mount Washington presents hazards comparable to far higher peaks due to its latitude, exposure, and weather patterns. The Sherman Adams summit building houses a museum, cafeteria, and meteorological observatory—infrastructure that makes the summit unusually developed compared with most major hiking peaks.
routeDescription: The Tuckerman Ravine Trail is a strenuous non-technical hike, best described as Class 1+ in normal summer conditions, with steep rock steps and boulder-strewn footing near the ravine and summit cone. The first 2.4 miles ascend through forest to Hermit Lake shelters (approximately 1,800 feet of gain), establishing a steady grade on well-maintained trail. From Hermit Lake, the route steepens as it enters Tuckerman Ravine itself, a classic glacial cirque bounded by steep headwalls. The final approach to the summit cone involves rock-hopping over angular boulders, with the steepest pitches occurring in the final few hundred feet. Total round-trip distance is 8.2–8.6 miles; experienced parties typically complete this in 5–10 hours depending on conditions and fitness.

Other established approaches include the Lion Head Trail (steeper and rougher, approximately 3.6 miles to summit), the Ammonoosuc Ravine Trail (a shorter but steep and rugged west-side approach), and the Huntington Ravine Trail (involving exposed scrambling, appropriate only for experienced hikers with existing scrambling competency). The summit is also accessible via the Mount Washington Cog Railway and Auto Road—an alternative to hiking for parties wishing to avoid the ascent.
routeLandmarks: Hermit Lake Shelters (approximately 1,800 ft gain, ~2.4 miles from Pinkham Notch)
Tuckerman Ravine floor/headwall area — seasonal snow, ice, avalanche, and closure concerns
Summit cone — rough rock steps and boulder-strewn trail above treeline
content.permitsClimbing: No special climbing permit is required to ascend Mount Washington. Parking, recreation-pass requirements, and facility rules vary by trailhead and managing organization, including White Mountain National Forest and AMC-operated facilities in the area. Verify current fees and pass requirements before departure. Services at Pinkham Notch Visitor Center and on the summit vary seasonally; verify current hours before relying on food, water, restrooms, or indoor shelter.
content.permitsCamping: Hermit Lake Shelters provide managed backcountry camping below Tuckerman Ravine, with lean-tos, tent platforms, outhouses, seasonal water access, and caretaker-managed procedures. Individual campers purchase permits at AMC Pinkham Notch Visitor Center; confirm current booking procedures and stay limits before planning. The AMC Lakes of the Clouds Hut (5,012 feet) offers seasonal bunk accommodations with meals; reservations required during full-service season. The Harvard Cabin operates as a winter facility with limited cabin and tent-site accommodations; confirm current dates and procedures before planning. Above-treeline camping is generally prohibited. The winter exception applies only when at least two feet of snow covers the ground, and it does not allow camping in the Cutler River Drainage, including Tuckerman and Huntington Ravines.
content.wildlife: The Tuckerman Ravine Trail is non-technical with relentless elevation gain and a sustained moderate-to-steep grade. The route remains a trail hike in normal summer conditions, but the summit cone and upper ravine sections involve rough footing, rock steps, and boulder travel. Alternative routes vary significantly in difficulty: Lion Head Trail is steeper and rougher, while Huntington Ravine Trail involves exposed scrambling and is appropriate only for experienced hikers with existing scrambling competency. Mount Washington's weather is the primary hazard. The peak is known colloquially as the "Home of the World's Worst Weather" and experiences severe storms, including snow and sleet, during any month of the year. Winds can exceed 60 mph even in summer, and the combination of wind, cold, and wetness can exhaust strong hikers. More than 100 deaths have been documented on and around Mount Washington, with some counts approaching or exceeding 150 depending on the area and date range included. A rapid deterioration of conditions at higher elevations can leave parties exposed and unable to descend safely. Parties should carry adequate insulation and rain protection regardless of forecast conditions and be prepared to turn back if conditions worsen.
content.footnote: Elevation 6,288 ft (traditional public figure); 6,286.5 ft NAVD88 may appear in technical sources. Prominence approximately 6,148 ft. Wind record: 231 mph, April 12, 1934. Parking, recreation-pass, and facility fees vary by trailhead; verify current AMC, WMNF, and MWAC requirements before travel.

permitFee: Free; parking, recreation pass, or facility fees may apply by trailhead
permitSeason: No climbing permit for day hiking; parking, recreation pass, and facility fees vary by trailhead (AMC/WMNF)

name: Mount Mitchell
dataSource: USGS, NC State Parks, ListsOfJohn; PeakBagger/SummitPost (route, class, hazards, permits)
safetyNote: Above 5,000 feet, fog and wind exposure increase markedly, and travel can feel significantly more severe than lower sections despite remaining on trail. Dense fog, cold rain, and strong winds can occur in any season; carry insulating layers and a waterproof shell.

overview: Mount Mitchell is the highest peak east of the Mississippi River, rising 6,684 feet above North Carolina's Black Mountains roughly 30 miles northeast of Asheville. With approximately 6,090 feet of prominence, it ranks among the most isolated summits in the eastern United States. The peak occupies Mount Mitchell State Park, a rare remnant of the Southern Appalachian spruce-fir forest dominated by red spruce and Fraser fir at elevations above 4,500 feet.

The summit marks a distinct ecological boundary. Visiting hikers encounter conditions characteristic of far northern forests despite the latitude, with winter snowfall averaging 104 inches and recorded temperatures dropping to −34°F. Dense fog, cold rain, and strong winds can occur in any season, making this an unusually severe mountain environment for the eastern slope of the Appalachians.
routeDescription: The Mount Mitchell Trail is the standard and most heavily traveled non-motorized route to the summit, ascending from Black Mountain Campground. The trail gains approximately 3,600 to 3,700 feet over 11.2 to 12 miles round-trip on sustained but well-maintained footpath. The route begins near Black Mountain Campground at roughly 2,995 feet, follows gentle grades through deciduous forest in the lower sections, then steepens as switchbacks climb toward the high ridge. The physical crux is the relentless sustained climbing with minimal rest sections, particularly in the upper reaches where the trail enters the dense spruce-fir forest. Above 5,000 feet, fog and wind exposure increase markedly, and travel can feel significantly more severe than lower sections despite remaining on trail. The route finishes at the developed summit area, where a short path leads to the observation deck.
The Black Mountain Crest Trail offers a significantly more strenuous alternative, traversing the high ridge over terrain requiring strong fitness and comfort with steep, rugged footing.
content.permitsClimbing: No climbing permit is required for day hiking Mount Mitchell. Mount Mitchell State Park charges no day-use entrance fee for vehicles parking outside the summit area. However, the Mount Mitchell State Park campground at Black Mountain Campground is closed for the entire 2025 and 2026 seasons due to water line installation. Verify current access and campground status before planning an overnight trip, as operations are subject to seasonal closures and maintenance.
content.permitsCamping: The Mount Mitchell State Park campground at Black Mountain Campground is currently closed for the entire 2025 and 2026 seasons due to water line installation and will reopen in 2027. When the campground operates in future years, non-electric tent camping costs $27 per night and requires advance reservation through Reserve America. Backcountry camping rules vary by jurisdiction; confirm current registration or permit requirements with the relevant land manager before departure. Water is available from springs in the lower sections of the trail during normal conditions. Upper-elevation water sources are unreliable; parties should carry sufficient water from lower elevations. Fire regulations vary by location and season; confirm current requirements with the park office before camping. Black bears are present in the region; inquire at the visitor center regarding current food storage protocols and bear-awareness guidelines.
content.wildlife: Peak season runs May through October. Winter snowfall averages 104 inches at the summit, with recorded temperatures dropping to −34°F. Dense fog, cold rain, and strong winds can occur in any season, making this an unusually severe mountain environment for the eastern slope of the Appalachians despite the moderate summit elevation.
content.footnote: Stats sources: USGS and NC State Parks (elevation 6,684 ft); ListsOfJohn (prominence 6,090 ft); NC State Parks Mount Mitchell Trail description (11.2–12 miles round-trip, 3,600–3,700 ft gain). Campground closed 2025–2026 for water line installation; reopens 2027. Future non-electric tent sites $27/night via Reserve America (rates subject to change).

permitSeason: No climbing permit required; Black Mountain Campground closed 2025–2026 (reopens 2027)

name: Humphreys Peak
dataSource: Coconino National Forest, SummitPost, U.S. Highpoint Guide, Arizona Highways
permitSeason: No permit required for day use; verify winter road access and any seasonal requirements with Coconino National Forest
safetyNote: Afternoon thunderstorms build rapidly during the July through September monsoon season, with lightning posing a documented fatality risk on the exposed upper ridge. During monsoon season, many parties start at or before dawn and aim to be off the exposed ridge before noon.

overview: Arizona's highest summit and one of the southernmost ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States, Humphreys Peak reaches 12,633 feet at the apex of the San Francisco Peaks volcanic field northwest of Flagstaff. The peak is the remnant high point of a massive stratovolcano estimated to have exceeded 15,000 to 16,000 feet before collapse and erosion reshaped the range. What remains is a broad volcanic massif whose upper slopes hold the only alpine tundra ecosystem in Arizona, a fragile environment that drives the peak's most distinctive land-management restrictions.

The San Francisco Peaks support several endemic plant species found nowhere else on Earth, including the San Francisco Peaks groundsel, concentrated in the tundra zone above 11,400 feet. That fragility has produced strict rules against off-trail travel and camping above treeline. The range name is generally associated with Spanish Franciscan naming traditions honoring Saint Francis of Assisi. Humphreys Peak was named for Brigadier General Andrew A. Humphreys, a 19th-century Army engineer associated with the western surveys of the period.
routeDescription: The standard route departs the Arizona Snowbowl lower parking lot at 9,333 feet and gains elevation steadily through ponderosa pine and mixed conifer forest for the first 3 miles, with switchbacks and sections of exposed tree roots and loose stone. At approximately 11,400 feet, a posted sign marks the upper camping boundary; the trail continues to Agassiz Saddle at 11,780 feet, a narrow notch separating Humphreys from Agassiz Peak to the south and the junction with the Weatherford Trail.

Above the saddle, forest gives way abruptly to open alpine terrain with wind-stunted trees, exposed rock, and low-growing tundra vegetation. The final mile to the summit traverses a series of false summits on Class 2 volcanic talus and scree, with the route fully exposed to wind and weather. The summit at 12,633 feet is marked by a wooden post and rough stone windbreak. Round-trip distance is 9.6 miles; total elevation gain is 3,300 feet.
content.permitsClimbing: No permit is required for day-use hiking on Humphreys Peak Trail. In winter, road access and wilderness permit requirements can vary with Arizona Snowbowl operations, storm conditions, and Forest Service decisions. Verify current road status and any permit requirements with Coconino National Forest before attempting a winter ascent.
content.permitsCamping: Camping above 11,400 feet is prohibited under a standing Forest Service occupancy order designed to protect the San Francisco Peaks alpine tundra ecosystem. Below that elevation, dispersed camping is permitted on Coconino National Forest land subject to current closure orders and Leave No Trace practices. The Flagstaff area has a history of fire-related closure orders that can affect dispersed camping zones on short notice. Hart Prairie, located below the San Francisco Mountain massif, is a dispersed camping area; check current Forest Service status before planning a stay. Lockett Meadow Campground, on the mountain's northeast side, is reached by a separate road and is typically seasonal with fees; road access and site availability change annually, so verify current Coconino National Forest status before relying on it. No water is available on the Humphreys Peak Trail itself; all water must be carried from the trailhead. The Snowbowl lodge at the parking area may have restrooms and water during operating hours but is not on-trail.
content.wildlife: Peak season runs May through October. July through September monsoon season brings rapid afternoon thunderstorm development and elevated lightning risk on the exposed upper ridge. December through May may hold snow and ice on the upper mountain, requiring traction and winter travel skills. Off-trail travel is prohibited above treeline to protect rare endemic tundra species.
content.footnote: Trailhead: Arizona Snowbowl lower parking lot, near 9300 N Snow Bowl Rd, Flagstaff, AZ 86001.

name: Borah Peak
dataSource: Salmon–Challis National Forest, USGS GNIS, USGS Earthquake Hazards; Idaho: A Climbing Guide, Sawtooth Mountain Guides, SummitPost, U.S. Highpoint Guide
safetyNote: A very early start, often 3–4 AM, is strongly recommended in summer. Plan to summit early and be descending well before afternoon thunderstorm development, which is common July through August. The exposed ridge presents significant lightning risk.

overview: Borah Peak at 12,662 feet is Idaho's highest mountain and the most prominent peak in the state, standing well above the surrounding basin landscape of central Idaho. The peak anchors the Lost River Range, which contains most of Idaho's 12,000-foot peaks. Named for U.S. Senator William Borah, the mountain rises as the dominant feature in a region characterized by sparse vegetation and high-desert conditions.

The peak is best known for Chickenout Ridge, an exposed Class 3 crux between 11,200 and 11,400 feet where the psychological exposure often exceeds the actual technical difficulty. The ridge is narrow, features loose rock, and drops steeply on the north flank. Many experienced climbers choose to turn back here despite solid route-finding skills. The October 1983 Borah Peak earthquake uplifted the Lost River fault by several feet; modern published elevations commonly cite 12,662 feet, though topographic surveys have some variation.
routeDescription: The Southwest Ridge is the standard and most-traveled route to the summit. The climb begins at Borah Peak Trailhead at 7,400 feet and gains approximately 5,200 to 5,300 feet over roughly 8 to 8.2 miles round-trip. The lower section ascends steep sagebrush-covered slopes on an established trail, reaching open alpine terrain by mid-elevation. From there, the route follows the southwest ridge spine upward through increasingly exposed scrambling terrain.

The Chickenout Ridge crux occupies the narrow section between 11,200 and 11,400 feet. This Class 3 section requires solid scrambling technique over loose rock, careful footwork, and comfort with significant exposure. The north flank drops steeply toward the basin below, creating serious exposure. Parties must navigate route variations around the ridge crest, all of which demand careful attention to foot placement and route-finding in order to avoid rockfall hazard to climbers below. Above the crux, parties continue on scree and talus slopes to reach the bare rock summit, which offers expansive views across the Lost River Range, Lemhi Mountains, and surrounding peaks to the south and east.

Descent follows the same route; the Chickenout Ridge presents the same technical challenge on the way down. Early-season snow may make glissading appealing but adds significant risk; downclimbing the Class 3 section is the safer choice.
routeLandmarks: Chickenout Ridge (11,200–11,400 ft) — exposed Class 3 scrambling with serious exposure on the north flank
Summit (12,662 ft) — bare rock with expansive alpine views
content.permitsClimbing: No climbing permit or quota is required for Borah Peak. Verify current road access, fire restrictions, and any temporary Forest Service closures before travel by contacting Salmon–Challis National Forest.
content.permitsCamping: The Borah Peak Trailhead, located 2.5 miles from Highway 93, has a primitive first-come campground and vault toilet. No reliable potable water is available at the trailhead or along the route. Parties should arrive with all water needed for the climb and trailhead camping. The steep terrain above 7,400 feet offers little practical camping; day climbing from the trailhead is standard. Dispersed camping exists nearby if the trailhead campground is full. Contact Salmon–Challis National Forest for current campground details, site availability, and seasonal access before travel.
content.wildlife: July through early September offers the most stable and snow-free conditions. Late June can be snow-dependent. September brings shorter days and colder conditions, with snow possible later in the month. May and June involve snow; early-season ascents require winter mountaineering competency. Downclimbing is safer than glissading on early-season snow. The sustained elevation gain of 5,200 to 5,300 feet demands strong fitness. Class 3 scrambling requires comfort with hands-and-feet movement on loose, exposed rock. Mountain navigation in poor visibility and sound turnaround discipline are essential. Ropes and climbing protection are not required in dry summer conditions. Snow-covered ascents, common in May and June and possible into July or again in September, are significantly more technical. Steep snowfields, potential avalanche terrain on the north face, and narrow snow ridges require an ice axe, crampons, and solid winter mountaineering skills with self-arrest ability. Afternoon thunderstorm development is typical July through August; the exposed ridge presents extreme lightning risk. The rapid ascent to 12,662 feet may cause altitude sickness; adequate acclimatization is important. Despite its Class 3 technical grade, Borah ranks among the most challenging state highpoints due to sustained elevation gain, altitude, exposure, and the mental discipline required to turn back if conditions warrant.
content.footnote: Location: Lost River Range, Idaho — Custer County; Salmon–Challis National Forest. Primary sources: Salmon–Challis National Forest, USGS GNIS, USGS Earthquake Hazards (1983 Borah Peak earthquake). Route and community sources: Idaho: A Climbing Guide, Sawtooth Mountain Guides, SummitPost, U.S. Highpoint Guide.

permitSeason: No climbing permit required; primitive first-come trailhead campground at Borah Peak Trailhead

name: Deseret Peak
bestRoute: Mill Fork Trail from Loop Campground
dataSource: Uinta-Wasatch-Cache National Forest, USGS, ListsOfJohn; PeakBagger/SummitPost (route, class, hazards)
safetyNote: False summits near the peak can create navigation hazards on descent. Before descending, identify the main summit cairn carefully to avoid drifting onto adjacent ridge systems or following false summit terrain. Cloud cover can shroud summit areas while surrounding desert basins remain clear, reducing visibility for route-finding.

overview: Deseret Peak stands at 11,031 feet as the highest point of Tooele County and the dominant summit of the Deseret Peak Wilderness, a 25,500-acre protected area in the Stansbury Range west of Salt Lake City. The peak rises prominently from surrounding high-desert basins, making it a major landmark in western Utah. Congress designated the wilderness area in 1984, preserving the transition zone between the low valleys of the Great Salt Lake Desert and the forested slopes above 8,000 feet.

The standard route up Deseret Peak is short enough for a day hike but steep enough to demand real effort. The landscape—aspen groves, alpine meadows, and loose rocky slopes—is typical of mountain ranges west of the Wasatch, but shaped by the drier climate of the Great Basin. From the summit, the view extends west across the Bonneville Salt Flats and east into the Wasatch Mountains; on clear days, distant landmarks include Pilot Peak nearly 60 miles northwest in Nevada and Mount Nebo roughly 60 miles southeast. The peak receives far less visitation than comparable summits in the Wasatch Range, making it an attractive objective for those pursuing the Ultra-57 list.
routeDescription: The Mill Fork Trail is the standard and most direct route to Deseret Peak. The route covers 7.8 miles round-trip, gaining 3,613 feet over 5 to 7 hours of hiking. The trail begins at the Mill Fork Trailhead near Loop Campground at approximately 7,418 feet, climbs through aspen forest for roughly 0.75 miles, and crosses South Willow Creek before reaching a junction. The left fork continues toward Deseret Peak; the right branch leads to Willow Lakes. Past the junction, the trail enters Mill Fork valley and climbs steeply through meadows with increasingly open views of Deseret Peak's southwest face. At the head of the canyon, the trail reaches a saddle and then ascends via switchbacks on loose, rocky terrain before gaining the summit ridge and reaching the peak. The descent follows the same route.
content.permitsClimbing: No climbing permit is required to ascend Deseret Peak. Campground fees apply for overnight stays at Loop Campground; verify current day-use parking and access fees before travel.
content.permitsCamping: Developed campsites at Loop Campground near the trailhead offer vault restrooms; potable water may not be available, so campers should bring all drinking water. Upper Narrows and Lower Narrows campgrounds, also managed by the Forest Service, provide additional overnight options accessible from South Willow Canyon Road on a first-come, first-served basis. Overnight camping within Deseret Peak Wilderness is governed by Forest Service wilderness regulations. Current rules cover group-size limits, campsite setbacks from water sources and trails, fire restrictions, and prohibitions on motorized and mechanized equipment. Verify specific requirements with the Salt Lake Ranger District before overnight travel, as regulations may change seasonally.
content.wildlife: The Mill Fork Trail is classified as YDS Class 1 hiking, but the upper mountain features rough, loose, and steeply graded terrain that slows travel and demands careful footing. Loose scree covers much of the upper slopes and especially affects the descent. Parties consistently report the difficulty of route-finding on the descent due to the presence of multiple false summits; identifying the main peak before heading down is critical to avoid wasting time and effort. There is no reliable water available along the Mill Fork Trail in typical summer conditions. Hikers should carry all drinking water from the trailhead and plan accordingly. Snow patches may persist in the Mill Fork drainage into early summer, often around mid-June. Afternoon thunderstorms and weather changes are common at this elevation; summit conditions can differ significantly from weather in the surrounding desert basins.
content.footnote: Summit coordinates approximately 40.45948°N, 112.62630°W. Mill Fork Trailhead near Loop Campground approximately 40.48431°N, 112.60589°W. Elevation 11,031 ft; prominence 5,807 ft. Verify coordinates against current map, GPS track, or Forest Service source before travel.

permitSeason: No climbing permit required; campground fees apply at Loop Campground

name: Mount Jefferson (OR)
dataSource: USGS Mount Jefferson; Willamette National Forest; Recreation.gov Central Cascades Wilderness Permit; Washington Trails Association trip reports
permitSeason: Central Cascades Wilderness Permit required June 15–October 15: $1/person day use, $6/group overnight via Recreation.gov; federal recreation pass required at Pamelia Lake Trailhead
callout: Route character changes substantially with snowpack. Late June to mid-July is generally preferred when snowfields are consolidated and firm. Late-season conditions on bare rock involve sections reported as low 5th class. Soft afternoon snow on the traverse has contributed to serious accidents; an early start from high camp is standard practice. Check current trip reports and ranger-district notices before committing to the climb. This route description is not a substitute for a map, GPS track, current conditions report, and competent alpine route-finding.

overview: Oregon's second-highest peak, Mount Jefferson rises to 10,495–10,497 feet in the central Cascade Range, with exact elevation varying by source. Unlike the broader, less technical volcanic summits the state is better known for, Jefferson ends in a steep, exposed summit pinnacle where snow, ice, and loose volcanic rock combine with Class 4 scrambling to produce serious alpine consequences. With roughly 5,770 feet of topographic prominence, Jefferson ranks among Oregon's most isolated high peaks.

Mount Jefferson is a deeply eroded stratovolcano with no known Holocene eruptions; USGS places its most recent eruptive activity during the last major glaciation, culminating approximately 15,000 years ago. Its flanks hold numerous snow and ice features, including four glaciers consistently named on USGS maps: Whitewater, Jefferson Park, Russell, and Waldo. The surrounding Mount Jefferson Wilderness encompasses approximately 111,000 acres and more than 150 lakes, with Pamelia Lake serving as a common access point for the standard southwest-side climbing route. The eastern side of the mountain lies near the Warm Springs Indian Reservation boundary; public climbing access generally uses west- and north-side national forest approaches.
routeDescription: The standard approach begins at Pamelia Lake Trailhead (3,100 ft) and follows the trail to Pamelia Lake (4,900 ft), then continues toward Hunts Cove and higher camps below the South Ridge. Shale Lake (5,800 ft) is a common overnight camp for South Ridge parties, with established durable sites in the area and reliable seasonal water. From Shale Lake, a climbers' trail gains the South Ridge through loose volcanic sand and intermittent snowfields before reaching Red Saddle (approximately 9,900 ft), the key notch immediately below the summit block.

The final 400–600 feet vary sharply with conditions. In snow-covered conditions, parties often make an exposed snowfield traverse with severe runout below, potentially involving a bergschrund, rimaye, or moat crossing. When the snow has melted back, the route may shift onto direct scrambling on loose volcanic rock around the summit pinnacle. Both options require confident movement on exposed terrain, plus sound judgment about whether to rope up. The summit block is commonly described as Class 4 when snow and ice link the upper route, with low-5th loose volcanic rock possible in late-season bare conditions.

Alternative approaches exist via Jefferson Park from the north (Whitewater Trailhead) and via the Milk Creek drainage on the northwest, both involving additional mileage with similar technical demands above the snowline.
content.permitsClimbing: The Pamelia Lake Trailhead is managed under the Central Cascades Wilderness Permit system. From June 15 through October 15, day-use permits are required for entry at this trailhead ($1 per person), and overnight permits are required for all overnight trips in the Mount Jefferson Wilderness ($6 per group reservation fee). Permits are reserved through Recreation.gov; fees and release windows are subject to change and should be verified before departure at fs.usda.gov or Recreation.gov. A federal recreation pass (America the Beautiful or equivalent) is also required at the Pamelia Lake Trailhead. Demand at Pamelia Lake can fill on peak summer weekends; advance booking is advisable for July entries.
content.permitsCamping: Shale Lake is commonly used by South Ridge parties; Jefferson Park Scout Lake (5,900 ft) serves as an alternative for parties approaching from the north. Camp at least 100 feet from water sources. Campfires are not allowed above 5,700 feet and may be subject to additional seasonal restrictions; use a backpacking stove. Store food securely in a bear-resistant canister or by a proper food-hanging method where allowed; pack out all food scraps and trash. Group size is limited to 12 in designated areas.
content.wildlife: Late June to mid-July is generally the preferred climbing window when snowfields are consolidated and firm. Late-season bare-rock conditions increase technical difficulty and rockfall exposure. Black bears inhabit the Mount Jefferson Wilderness; use bear-resistant food storage and keep a clean camp.
content.footnote: Trailhead: Pamelia Lake Trailhead, accessed from Highway 22 via Pamelia Creek Road (Forest Road 2246), approximately 12.5 miles east of Detroit, Oregon. Verify current road conditions with the Willamette National Forest before departure.

name: South Sister
dataSource: Deschutes National Forest, Recreation.gov Central Cascades Wilderness Permit, USGS Cascades Volcano Observatory, Washington Trails Association trip reports
permitSeason: Central Cascades Wilderness Permit required June 15–October 15: $1/person day use, $6/group overnight via Recreation.gov; Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful Pass required at Devil's Lake Trailhead
safetyNote: The upper mountain above treeline is fully exposed. Afternoon convective storms are common around South Sister from July through August. Most experienced parties aim to reach the summit by midday and clear the crater rim by early afternoon to stay ahead of lightning risk.

overview: South Sister is the highest of the Three Sisters volcanic trio and the third-highest summit in Oregon. It rises to 10,363 feet above the conifer forests of the Deschutes National Forest. Its roughly 5,590 feet of topographic prominence make it one of Oregon's most prominent Cascade summits, and the mountain stands as the dominant summit of the Three Sisters Wilderness, a nearly 286,000-acre roadless area straddling the Deschutes and Willamette national forests. Geologically, South Sister is the youngest of the three volcanoes; its most recent eruption occurred about 2,000 years ago, and the USGS has documented earthquake swarms and ground uplift southwest of the summit in recent decades, though this has not indicated an imminent eruptive threat.

The mountain supports several glaciers, including Prouty Glacier, widely cited as Oregon's largest glacier. At the summit crater sits Teardrop Pool, commonly cited as the highest lake in Oregon. Unlike North Sister and many snowy-season routes on Middle Sister, South Sister's standard summer route is nontechnical and accessible to fit hikers in good conditions, which accounts for its status as one of the most sought-after day-use permits in the Central Cascades Wilderness system.
routeDescription: The standard route departs from Devil's Lake Trailhead at approximately 5,480 feet. Trail access from the trailhead area now uses the Elk-Devils Trail highway underpass rather than a direct highway crossing; check current trailhead instructions on Recreation.gov before departure. From the trailhead, the route climbs through conifer forest for roughly two miles, gaining about 1,400 feet before the trees open onto a broad sandy volcanic plateau. Broken Top and the full south face of South Sister come into view here.

From the plateau, the trail continues northeast across open terrain past Moraine Lake junction (approximately mile 3.2, 7,400 ft) before pitching steeply toward the Lewis Glacier terminal moraine at roughly 8,900 feet. The upper mountain is direct and unrelenting, with few meaningful switchbacks and a sustained grade on loose volcanic scree that gains roughly 1,500 feet to the crater rim at approximately 10,200 feet. A short traverse right along the crater rim reaches the summit. Most parties complete the 12–13 mile round trip in 8–10 hours.

Alternative access routes include the Green Lakes Trailhead to the north, which adds mileage but draws from a separate permit quota, and Todd Lake Trailhead, also an option when Devil's Lake permits are sold out.
routeLandmarks: Lewis Glacier terminal moraine (~8,900 ft, ~4.7 mi)
Summit crater and true summit high point (10,363 ft)
content.permitsClimbing: South Sister falls within the Central Cascades Wilderness Permit area, administered by the Deschutes National Forest. Permits are required from June 15 through October 15. Day-use permits are issued per person at $1 per person per entry; overnight permits cover groups of up to 12 at $6 per group. Permits are not available at the trailhead or at Forest Service offices; they must be reserved through Recreation.gov. Day-use permits are released in 10-day and 2-day rolling windows at 7:00 AM Pacific time daily. Overnight permit release dates and inventory distribution vary by year; Recreation.gov posts the current season's release schedule before the permit window opens. Weekday dates and shoulder-season windows in late June and early October are usually less competitive than peak summer weekends. A Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful Pass is required for trailhead parking. Permit quotas, fees, and release windows can change; confirm details on Recreation.gov before planning a trip.
content.permitsCamping: Overnight permit holders are generally not assigned designated campsites, but must follow all wilderness regulations, closure areas, group-size limits, and required setbacks from lakes, streams, and trails. Moraine Lake (approximately 7,400 ft) is the primary on-route overnight area and is managed as a designated camping basin; overnight campers must use posted sites and leave the basin if all designated sites are full. Green Lakes Basin, a viable alternative for parties approaching from the north, operates under the same designated-site rules. Campfires are prohibited above 5,700 feet; use a stove for all camps above treeline. The summit crater rim has informal rock windbreaks, but overnight camping there is exposed, environmentally fragile, and not recommended. Maximum overnight stay is 14 consecutive days. Groups of up to 12 are permitted on an overnight permit.
content.wildlife: July through September is the standard climbing season. May through June ascents may require crampons and ice axe above 8,000 feet. Afternoon convective storms are common in July and August; plan an early start and aim to clear the exposed upper mountain before early afternoon. The standard route does not require glacier travel; avoid entering glacier terrain without proper equipment and training.
content.footnote: Trailhead: Devil's Lake Trailhead (N 43.9793°, W 121.7415°), Deschutes National Forest. Access via Cascade Lakes Highway, which is seasonally closed in winter and spring; confirm road status before shoulder-season trips.

name: Pikes Peak
dataSource: City of Manitou Springs, Pike National Forest, Barr Camp, SummitPost, PeakBagger, trip reports
permitSeason: No climbing permit required; Barr Trailhead parking fee typically $25–$32.50 per day (reserve in peak season)
safetyNote: Afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer, and the upper route is exposed above treeline. Start early, set a firm turnaround time, and aim to be descending from the summit area by late morning or midday. Barr Camp can be a useful place to ask about recent conditions, but hikers should make independent weather decisions.

overview: Pikes Peak is one of Colorado's most prominent Front Range fourteeners and the dominant summit of the Pikes Peak massif. Rising directly above Colorado Springs and Manitou Springs, it forms the first major wall of the Rockies above the Great Plains and is visible for long distances across eastern Colorado. The mountain is unusually accessible for a fourteener: a road and cog railway reach the summit, and the top has developed visitor facilities. The Barr Trail, however, remains a serious hiking ascent. From Manitou Springs, the route gains 7,450 feet over 13 miles to the summit, passing from foothills forest to alpine tundra before finishing on rocky high-elevation terrain.

Pikes Peak is also one of the most culturally recognizable mountains in the United States. It inspired the lyrics to "America the Beautiful," and its name honors Zebulon Pike, who described the peak during an 1806 expedition. The mountain's prominence, visibility, and accessibility have made it one of Colorado's best-known summits.
routeDescription: The Barr Trail is the standard hiking route to the summit. The route is 13 miles one-way with 7,450 feet of elevation gain. The ascent is relentless and well-defined, with the initial section featuring steady switchbacks through the "Ws" as the trail climbs Mount Manitou. No-Name Creek appears at roughly 5.5 miles and marks the transition from Mount Manitou to the base of Pikes Peak proper, often providing seasonal water. Most hikers cover the first 6.5 miles to Barr Camp in 2 to 4 hours, depending on fitness and acclimatization.

Barr Camp sits at 10,200 feet and is the main halfway landmark on the route, with overnight lodging by reservation and nearby filterable water. From Barr Camp, the route continues roughly 6.5 more miles to the summit, gaining nearly 4,000 additional feet. The trail remains defined but becomes increasingly exposed above treeline. The final high-elevation section passes through rocky, uneven terrain near the 16 Golden Stairs before reaching the summit area.

Because the summit is accessible by road and cog railway, some hikers arrange a ride down rather than descending the full Barr Trail; confirm transportation plans in advance rather than assuming space or availability.
routeLandmarks: No-Name Creek (~5.5 miles): Seasonal water source before treeline
Barr Camp (~6.5 miles, ~10,200 ft): Overnight lodge, day-use facility, and nearby filterable water
A-Frame Shelter (~12,098 ft): Unstaffed shelter below the upper mountain
16 Golden Stairs (~13,500+ ft): Final rocky section
content.permitsClimbing: No climbing permit is required for the standard Barr Trail ascent. Parking at the Barr Trailhead in Manitou Springs requires a fee; current rates are typically $25–$32.50 per day. Parking is limited and should be arranged in advance during peak season. Road, parking, and summit access rules can change; verify current conditions before traveling.
content.permitsCamping: Barr Camp, located at roughly 6.5 miles and 10,200 feet, is the main overnight stop on the Barr Trail. It operates by reservation and offers bunkhouse lodging, lean-tos, and day-use space; amenities vary by reservation type. Water must be filtered or treated from nearby sources. Dispersed camping is allowed on nearby Pike National Forest land where not otherwise posted. Camp at least 100 feet from water sources and trails, follow current fire restrictions, store food securely, and pack out all trash. Camping is limited to 14 days within any continuous 30-day period.
content.wildlife: Peak hiking season usually runs from June through September, depending on snowpack and storm patterns. July through early September generally offer the most reliable dry-trail conditions but also bring the highest thunderstorm risk. September can bring cooler temperatures and fewer hikers, though early snow becomes more likely. Winter and shoulder-season ascents require traction, winter layers, navigation skills, and weather judgment depending on conditions.
content.footnote: Standard trailhead: Barr Trailhead, Manitou Springs. Parking reservations and fees apply during peak season; verify current trailhead access and summit transportation options before traveling.

permitFee: Free (no climbing permit; trailhead parking fee applies)

name: Mount Timpanogos
dataSource: Uinta-Wasatch-Cache National Forest, SummitPost, PeakBagger, Recreation.gov, trip reports
permitSeason: No climbing permit required; timed Timpooneke trailhead parking on summer weekends and holidays via Recreation.gov
safetyNote: An early start is strongly recommended, especially on weekends when parking fills early. Afternoon thunderstorms develop rapidly; plan a turnaround time that allows descent off the exposed ridge before early afternoon.

overview: Mount Timpanogos is the second-highest peak in Utah's Wasatch Range and one of the classic summit hikes of northern Utah. Rising more than 7,000 feet above Utah Valley, the mountain dominates the skyline from Provo, Orem, and much of the Wasatch Front. Its prominence is obvious from below: Timpanogos rises as a distinct massif above the valley rather than disappearing into the surrounding crest. With 5,270 feet of clean prominence, it ranks 47th among ultra-prominent peaks in the contiguous United States.

Timpanogos is a mountain of basins, cliffs, meadows, snowfields, and broken limestone. Two main trails reach the summit area, with Timpooneke generally serving as the standard route. The hike is not technical in normal summer conditions, but it is long, high, exposed, and popular. Strong fitness, an early start, and close attention to weather are the main requirements.

Mount Timpanogos is a Class 2 hike that requires strong fitness, comfort with altitude, and basic alpine judgment. The sustained elevation gain and summit elevation create a fitness-based challenge rather than a technical one. Route-finding is generally straightforward in normal summer conditions, though the final summit section requires comfort on rocky terrain and light scrambling. No ropes or climbing equipment are required for the standard summer route.
routeDescription: The Timpooneke Trail is the standard route, rising 14 miles round-trip with 4,389 feet of elevation gain. The route departs from Timpooneke Campground at about 7,370 feet, follows a well-marked path through alternating forest and open meadows, and gains elevation steadily toward the upper mountain.

Above the basin, the route climbs to Timpanogos Saddle at approximately 11,050 feet, a natural rest point with open views of Utah Valley and the surrounding peaks. The final 0.8 miles involve rocky Class 2 terrain and talus on the west side of the summit ridge, with about 700 feet of elevation gain. The route remains well-marked in normal summer conditions and does not require technical climbing. The main difficulty is the sustained gain, length, altitude, and exposure rather than any single technical crux.
routeLandmarks: Lower Basin: forest and meadow travel below the upper mountain
Upper Basin meadow: broad alpine basin below the saddle
Summit ridge: rocky Class 2 terrain with weather exposure
content.permitsClimbing: No climbing permit is required for the standard summer route. Mount Timpanogos is within the Mount Timpanogos Wilderness, managed by the Uinta-Wasatch-Cache National Forest. Timed parking permits are required for the Timpooneke trailhead on Saturdays, Sundays, and holidays during the summer season, including Pioneer Day in July and Labor Day in September. Permits are available through Recreation.gov. Each reservation covers one private vehicle for a designated time slot. Day-use requirements, release schedules, and fees may change; verify current rules with the Forest Service and Recreation.gov before traveling.
permitsCamping: Overnight backcountry camping does not require a separate wilderness permit. Maximum group size is 15 persons. Timpooneke Campground, located near the trailhead, provides reservable family campsites.

Emerald Lake sits below the summit massif at approximately 10,390 feet and is more commonly reached from the Aspen Grove trailhead (about 5.1 miles one-way with roughly 3,500 feet of elevation gain). It can also be accessed as a longer side trip from the Timpooneke route. It is one of the most popular overnight areas on the mountain, with established sites nearby and a historic stone shelter. Dispersed camping is permitted in the wilderness area, subject to local setback rules from water sources, trails, and fragile meadow habitat.
content.wildlife: Peak hiking season usually runs from late June through early October, depending on snowpack and early fall storms. The mountain can hold snow well into summer, especially in basins, gullies, and shaded north-facing terrain. July and August generally offer the most reliable trail access but also bring crowds and afternoon thunderstorms. September and early October often have cooler temperatures and fewer hikers, though early fall snow and storms become increasingly likely. Winter ascents are possible, including via Everest Ridge, but require mountaineering skills and avalanche awareness.

permitFee: Free (timed parking permit required on weekends/holidays)

name: Mount Cleveland
dataSource: Glacier National Park, Recreation.gov, USGS, ListsOfJohn; PeakBagger/SummitPost (route, class, hazards)
safetyNote: Route-finding is difficult on the Stoney Indian Peaks traverse, particularly along the exposed east-side section. Parties commonly lose the route in poor visibility. Descending in deteriorating weather on loose, unstable rock is hazardous. Early-season attempts can involve lingering snow on narrow ledges, increasing avalanche and slip risk.

overview: Mount Cleveland is the highest peak in Glacier National Park and the tallest summit in Montana's Lewis Range, commonly listed at 10,466 feet. With prominence around 5,252 feet depending on the source, it is one of the most isolated and visually dominant peaks in the Northern Rockies. The mountain sits on the Continental Divide near Glacier's northern boundary, close to Waterton Lakes National Park in Canada.

Climbers approaching Mount Cleveland face one of the more logistically demanding ultra-prominent peaks in the Lower 48. International border proximity, limited backcountry permit availability, long wilderness approaches, and complex route-finding make this a serious objective for experienced and self-sufficient parties. Mount Cleveland is also associated with one of Glacier National Park's most serious mountaineering tragedies: a December 1969 avalanche on the west face killed five climbers.
routeDescription: The standard approach to Mount Cleveland follows the Stoney Indian Route via Stoney Indian Lake, roughly 28–32 miles round-trip depending on approach, with over 6,500 feet of cumulative elevation gain. Most parties complete the climb in 2 to 3 days. Parties commonly access the route via the Goat Haunt area, which may involve a Waterton Lake boat shuttle from Canada, or by approaching from the Kootenay Lake area. Both approaches converge near Stoney Indian Lake, the primary base camp for the route. From Stoney Indian Lake, the route traverses the exposed east side of the Stoney Indian Peaks before ascending the 3rd and 4th class summit terrain on loose scree, talus, and blocky rock. The traverse is narrow and exposed, with little margin for error. Route-finding difficulties are commonly reported, especially in poor visibility. The descent is punishing on loose terrain and requires careful navigation.

Other established approaches include the West Face Route, a serious alternative with significant avalanche exposure, the Southeast Face Route from Chief Mountain Customs via Glenns Lake and Whitecrow Glacier, and longer U.S.-based options via the Belly River Trail from Chief Mountain Customs, the Highline Trail from Logan Pass, or Many Glacier via Ptarmigan Tunnel. These alternatives generally require more time, stronger route-finding ability, and greater technical judgment than the Stoney Indian Route.
routeLandmarks: Stoney Indian Peaks east traverse, approximately 9,500–10,000 ft
content.permitsClimbing: Backcountry permits are required for overnight camping in Glacier National Park. Mount Cleveland is usually approached through highly competitive backcountry zones, and advance reservations through Recreation.gov are strongly recommended. Limited walk-up availability may exist, but parties should not rely on obtaining a same-day permit for this objective. Glacier National Park charges backcountry permit and per-person camping fees during the main season. Fee amounts, reservation windows, and walk-up procedures can change, so parties should confirm current requirements with Glacier National Park and Recreation.gov before planning an itinerary. Commercial guiding may be restricted and requires proper authorization. Climbers should assume they need to be self-sufficient unless they have confirmed access to a legally authorized guide service.
content.permitsCamping: Stoney Indian Lake is the designated backcountry campsite closest to the peak, but availability is limited. Parties unable to secure Stoney Indian Lake may need to plan alternative camps at Kootenay Lake, Goat Haunt, or other permitted backcountry sites. All parties must follow Glacier National Park food-storage regulations. Approved bear-resistant storage or designated food-storage infrastructure may be required depending on the campsite. Bear spray is strongly recommended and should be readily accessible. Campfire restrictions, group-size limits, and distance-from-water regulations apply; confirm current rules with park rangers when obtaining permits.
content.wildlife: The Stoney Indian Route involves sustained scrambling on unstable scree and talus, exposed traversing with little margin for error, and complex route-finding on and around the Stoney Indian Peaks. The summit terrain includes 3rd and 4th class scrambling on loose rock. Roped climbing is not typically required for experienced parties in dry summer conditions, but exposure, loose rock, and route-finding make the route serious. Route descriptions from experienced parties consistently mention disorientation on the exposed traverse and difficulty relocating the descent route under stress, fatigue, or poor visibility. Mount Cleveland sits in a severe alpine environment where weather can deteriorate quickly, even in midsummer. Early-season attempts, especially in June and early July, may encounter heavy snowpack on narrow ledges, creating avalanche danger and serious slip risk. Late-season attempts face shorter days, colder temperatures, and early winter storms. The best climbing window is usually late July through mid-September, when snow coverage is lower and weather is generally more stable. Winter and early-spring ascents are serious mountaineering objectives requiring advanced snow, avalanche, and cold-weather skills.
content.footnote: Elevation 10,466 ft (commonly cited; LiDAR-derived sources may vary slightly). Prominence approximately 5,252 ft (ListsOfJohn). Route mileage varies by approach (Goat Haunt, Belly River, Kootenai/Kintla). Confirm current Glacier NP backcountry permit windows, fees, and access procedures on NPS/Recreation.gov before travel. International travel logistics and Canadian border proximity may affect Waterton access.

permitFee: No climbing fee; entrance and backcountry permit fees apply
permitSeason: Backcountry permit required for overnight itineraries; advance Recreation.gov reservations strongly recommended

name: Arc Dome
dataSource: Humboldt-Toiyabe National Forest, USGS, ListsOfJohn; PeakBagger/SummitPost (route, class, hazards)
safetyNote: Afternoon thunderstorms are common in the Toiyabe Range during summer. Start early and aim to be off the summit ridge and exposed high crest before storms develop. Much of the upper route is open terrain with limited shelter from lightning, wind, or sudden temperature drops.

overview: Arc Dome reaches 11,778 feet in the central Nevada Great Basin and is one of the most prominent summits in the state. It is the highest point in the Toiyabe Range and one of central Nevada's defining high-country landmarks. The peak anchors the 120,551-acre Arc Dome Wilderness Area, where lush, alpine-like canyons contrast with desert valleys thousands of feet below.

The range's high elevation and exposed crest hold lingering snow into the shoulder seasons, creating a cooler high-country environment above the surrounding desert basins. From the summit, visibility extends across the central Great Basin and into California, including views of Mount Jefferson, the White Mountains, and surrounding ranges.
routeDescription: The standard route begins at Columbine Campground, approximately 8,600 feet, and follows the Columbine Trail toward Stewart Creek before reaching the Toiyabe Crest National Recreation Trail. The route climbs roughly 4,335 feet over 14.6 miles round-trip. Near the upper basin, the route turns toward Arc Dome and climbs through open meadows with broad views of the southwest face. The grade steepens near the saddle at the head of the canyon, where tight switchbacks lead toward the summit ridge. Above 11,000 feet, the trail crosses talus and loose rock, making progress slower despite the nontechnical terrain. The final section remains Class 1 hiking on an established trail.

Columbine Campground is remote, and the approach can take longer than mileage alone suggests. From US 50 west of Austin, Nevada, take State Route 722 and Route 21 via the Yomba Indian Reservation, then follow Forest Service Road 119 for approximately 10 miles to the campground. The final stretch is rough dirt road; high-clearance vehicles are recommended, and conditions can vary seasonally. Plan extra time for the drive and verify current road conditions with the Humboldt-Toiyabe National Forest before departing.
routeLandmarks: Summit ridge and Arc Dome (11,778 ft)
content.permitsClimbing: No climbing permit or fee is required for Arc Dome. Normal wilderness regulations apply to backcountry camping. The Arc Dome Wilderness Area prohibits motorized vehicles and mechanized transport, including bicycles.
content.permitsCamping: Columbine Campground trailhead offers free campsites with 7 camp units and functions as the primary access point. The campground may see heavier use during hunting season. Backcountry camping is possible along the route where durable sites and water access allow, but the Toiyabe Crest is exposed and often dry. Camp at least 100 feet from water sources. Fires may be restricted, especially near or above tree line and during dry periods; check current Forest Service regulations before your trip.
content.wildlife: The route is strenuous Class 1 hiking on established trails and loose upper-mountain tread. The crux follows tight switchbacks across loose rock and talus above 11,000 feet; while the route remains Class 1, the steep grade, unstable footing, and elevation make the final 1,000 vertical feet the most strenuous part of the climb. June through September offers the most reliable conditions. May and October are possible depending on snowpack and weather. Early-season and late-season ascents may require waterproof boots and gaiters due to lingering snow and wet conditions above 10,000 feet. Summer temperatures at the trailhead can be hot, and afternoon thunderstorms are frequent; parties should start early and aim to be off exposed high terrain before storms develop. The Toiyabe Crest has little reliable water; carry sufficient capacity and plan water sources carefully. Wild horses may be encountered in the Toiyabe Range and Arc Dome Wilderness, particularly near open meadows and along portions of the Toiyabe Crest Trail. Mule deer and sage grouse may also be seen. Treat or filter all backcountry water due to wildlife and grazing activity in the area.
content.footnote: Elevation commonly cited as 11,778 ft; sources vary between 11,773 and 11,778 ft. Prominence 5,213 ft (ListsOfJohn). Trailhead: Columbine Campground N 38.90009° W 117.37609°. Route stats per Humboldt-Toiyabe National Forest and standard trip reports (14.6 miles round-trip, 4,335 ft gain).

permitSeason: No climbing permit or fee; free Columbine Campground trailhead access
